summary: CC-0105 splits the keystone-operator controller off from the openstack workload Namespace into a dedicated keystone-system Namespace, retires the dead monitoring-system Namespace in favour of the live monitoring Namespace, documents the new Namespace policy in architecture/docs/05-deployment/04-namespace-policy.md, and adds a comprehensive set of guard tests (unit, helm-unittest, integration). The HelmRelease moves, Helm chart parametricity, kind suspend-patch retargeting, ci-deploy-operator.sh --namespace flag, observability/quick-start docs, infrastructure-manifests inventory, and four chainsaw suites (gateway-quick-start, httproute, metrics, network-policy-egress, semantic-invariants, both operator-pod chaos suites) were updated correctly. However, the cross-reference audit (Task 8.2) missed three production-impacting call sites that still pin the keystone-operator HelmRelease/ServiceMonitor to the old namespaces, regressing the WITH_PROMETHEUS=true deploy path and the e2e-prometheus CI matrix job.

tests_checklist.item: Unit tests for namespaces, helmrelease_namespaces, kind_overlay exist and follow assertions.sh style
Helm-unittest release_namespace_test.yaml asserts every namespaced resource lands in keystone-system
Architecture-doc test asserts policy file, link from index.md, all Namespaces, migration runbook keywords
Docs-consistency test asserts no stale monitoring-system, operator selector → keystone-system, workload selector → openstack
Integration tests (kustomize_render, full_kind_up) gate cleanly on missing tools (SKIP)
All operator-pod selectors in chainsaw suites repointed to keystone-system
All operator-HelmRelease lookups (catch-block diagnostics) repointed to keystone-system

code_quality_checklist.item: Feature ID (CC-0105) consistently appended to existing feature markers on changed files
SPDX headers preserved on every new test/script
DECISION comments capture intentional divergence (namespace-scoped-rbac test, kind/prometheus/namespace.yaml, kind/base/kustomization.yaml)
Stale DECISION/NOTE comments removed once fixtures align

security_checklist.item: No new secrets exposure; controller-vs-workload split improves blast-radius isolation
RBAC unchanged (ClusterRole/RoleBinding subjects updated to keystone-system via Helm release namespace, not by hand)

architecture_checklist.item: Workload (Keystone CR, Deployment, Secrets, HTTPRoute, NetworkPolicy in openstack) is unchanged — no workload churn
monitoring vs monitoring-system collapse is consistent (only carve-outs are migration notes / regression-guard tests)

dry_yagni_checklist.item: No new pattern introduced; existing FluxCD HelmRelease and kind-suspend-patch patterns followed
Architecture-doc migration runbook reuses the existing suspend → uninstall → annotation-cleanup → reinstall flow

fail_fast_checklist.item: Static contract tests (yq, awk-based) fail closed on namespace drift
Integration kustomize-render test catches orphan patch targets and duplicate Namespace renders

defensive_checklist.item: Integration tests SKIP cleanly when kustomize/yq/kind/kubectl/helm/make is missing instead of failing falsely
kind/prometheus/namespace.yaml DECISION explains the dual declaration (LoadRestrictionsRootOnly constraint)

architecture_findings.description: Stale `kubectl patch helmrelease keystone-operator -n openstack` in hack/deploy-infra.sh — after CC-0105 the HelmRelease lives in keystone-system, so the patch (and the subsequent `kubectl get -o jsonpath='{.spec.suspend}'` lookup) will fail to find the HR and the function will skip silently or error out. This breaks `WITH_PROMETHEUS=true make deploy-infra` and, by extension, the e2e-prometheus CI matrix job that gates monitoring.serviceMonitor.enabled via this helper.
architecture_findings.fix: Replace both `-n openstack` with `-n keystone-system` (and the matching unit-test assertion at tests/unit/hack/deploy_infra_prometheus_flag_test.sh:240). The kind suspend-patch comment block has already been updated to reflect that the HelmRelease now lives in keystone-system; these are the only two remaining call sites.
architecture_findings.evidence:
631:  kubectl patch helmrelease keystone-operator -n openstack --type=merge \
635:  suspended=$(kubectl get helmrelease keystone-operator -n openstack \
         -o jsonpath='{.spec.suspend}' 2>/dev/null || true)

issues_found.description: tests/e2e/keystone/prometheus-stack/chainsaw-test.yaml was not updated for CC-0105. Step 2 (`servicemonitor-exists`) hard-codes `kubectl get servicemonitor keystone-operator -n default`, but ci-deploy-operator.sh now installs into `keystone-system` (NAMESPACE default), so the rendered ServiceMonitor will live in `keystone-system`. Step 2 will fail on every e2e-prometheus run. The catch-block lookups (`kubectl get helmrelease keystone-operator -n openstack`) also target the old namespace. The explanatory comment block at lines 287-297 is now factually wrong — it claims helm is invoked without `--namespace`, which CC-0105 explicitly changed.
issues_found.fix: 1) Replace `-n default` with `-n keystone-system` on the servicemonitor query (line 298). 2) Replace `-n openstack` with `-n keystone-system` on both HelmRelease catch-block lookups (lines 307, 311). 3) Rewrite the comment block at lines 287-297 to reflect that hack/ci-deploy-operator.sh now passes `--namespace keystone-system --create-namespace`, so the ServiceMonitor renders in keystone-system. 4) Confirm that the metrics test in tests/e2e/keystone/metrics/chainsaw-test.yaml (already retargeted) and this prometheus-stack suite stay consistent.
issues_found.evidence:
298:          kubectl get servicemonitor keystone-operator -n default \
307:          kubectl get helmrelease keystone-operator -n openstack \
311:          kubectl get helmrelease keystone-operator -n openstack \

issues_found.description: tests/unit/hack/deploy_infra_prometheus_flag_test.sh:240 codifies the buggy expectation — it asserts that `hack/deploy-infra.sh` contains the literal string `kubectl patch helmrelease keystone-operator -n openstack`. Once the script is corrected to `-n keystone-system`, this test will fail. The unit test must be updated in lockstep with the script fix or the fix cannot land.
issues_found.fix: Update the assertion to expect `kubectl patch helmrelease keystone-operator -n keystone-system`. Same applies to any other assertion in this file referencing the openstack namespace for the operator HelmRelease.

issues_found.description: Stale doc example: docs/reference/infrastructure/infrastructure-manifests.md:807 shows `kubectl patch helmrelease keystone-operator -n openstack` as the documented way to enable ServiceMonitor. After CC-0105 the HelmRelease is in keystone-system; users copy-pasting this snippet will get a `helmreleases.helm.toolkit.fluxcd.io "keystone-operator" not found` error.
issues_found.fix: Replace `-n openstack` with `-n keystone-system` in the patch example. This is the same fix as the script bug (architecture_findings #1); keeping doc and script in sync prevents the docs from re-introducing the regression.

issues_found.description: Stale DECISION comment in docs/reference/testing/chaos-e2e-tests.md:533 reads: 'The chainsaw fixtures under tests/e2e-chaos/operator-pod-{crash,kill}/01-podchaos.yaml and hack/ci-deploy-operator.sh still target `default`; those will be retargeted in a sibling task (operator deploy script + fixtures) for CC-0105.' But this PR HAS already retargeted both files (the 01-podchaos.yaml files now have `selector.namespaces: [keystone-system]`, and ci-deploy-operator.sh installs into keystone-system by default). The comment is now actively misleading and contradicts the surrounding prose, which correctly says PodChaos targets `keystone-system`.
issues_found.fix: Remove the entire <!-- DECISION: ... --> HTML comment. The surrounding bullet already documents the correct post-CC-0105 state.
issues_found.evidence: <!-- DECISION: docs describe the post-CC-0105 target state (keystone-operator in keystone-system). The chainsaw fixtures under tests/e2e-chaos/operator-pod-{crash,kill}/01-podchaos.yaml and hack/ci-deploy-operator.sh still target `default`; those will be retargeted in a sibling task (operator deploy script + fixtures) for CC-0105. Flagged here to keep docs and fixtures self-consistent once both land. -->

issues_found.description: tests/e2e/infrastructure/no-prometheus-when-disabled/chainsaw-test.yaml has stale catch-block diagnostics — `kubectl get servicemonitor.monitoring.coreos.com -n openstack` (line 134) and `kubectl get helmrelease.helm.toolkit.fluxcd.io keystone-operator -n openstack` (line 136). These are diagnostic dumps on failure, not test logic, so the test still passes, but on a real failure these will produce empty/misleading output (no ServiceMonitor or HR exists in openstack post-CC-0105). This degrades CI-failure diagnosability — exactly the concern called out by review-pattern `ensure-test-error-capture-blocks-collect-sufficient-diagnostic-context`.
issues_found.fix: Update the catch-block diagnostic namespaces: ServiceMonitor lookup should query `keystone-system` (and arguably both for completeness), and the keystone-operator HelmRelease lookup should query `keystone-system`. Same drift root cause as the BLOCKERs above.
issues_found.evidence:
134:          kubectl get servicemonitor.monitoring.coreos.com -n openstack -o wide 2>&1 || true
136:          kubectl get helmrelease.helm.toolkit.fluxcd.io keystone-operator -n openstack \

issues_found.description: tests/integration/ci/full_kind_up_test.sh:91 invokes `make -C "$PROJECT_ROOT" kind-up`, but the Makefile defines no `kind-up` target (only `deploy-infra`, which runs `hack/deploy-infra.sh`). Whenever the test is run on a host without an existing kind cluster, the make invocation fails and the test reports FAIL even though kind tooling is healthy. The DoD acceptance criterion REQ-017 (`make kind-up passes from a clean cluster`) is also not satisfiable — `make kind-up` returns 2 (`No rule to make target 'kind-up'`).
issues_found.fix: Either (a) add a `kind-up` Makefile target that delegates to `hack/deploy-infra.sh` (matches REQ-017 verbiage in the plan) and use it consistently, or (b) update the test to call `hack/deploy-infra.sh` directly and revise REQ-017 in the plan to match.
issues_found.evidence:
if ! make -C "$PROJECT_ROOT" kind-up >"$log" 2>&1; then
      echo "  FAIL: make kind-up failed; last 50 lines of output:"

issues_found.description: Leftover review prompt in tests/e2e/keystone/namespace-scoped-rbac/chainsaw-test.yaml:18 — the DECISION comment ends with `Reviewer: please verify.` This is the exact pattern flagged in external-review pattern `resolve-ambiguous-commented-out-alternatives-before-merge` (and its sibling `inline reviewer-please-verify` from CC-0065). Once the namespace-scoped-rbac posture is reviewed and codified, the literal `Reviewer: please verify.` clause should be dropped so the comment reads as a durable design note, not an open question.
issues_found.fix: Drop the trailing `Reviewer: please verify.` from the DECISION comment, leaving the rationale as a permanent record of the controller-vs-workload-with-namespace-scoped-RBAC carve-out.
issues_found.evidence: # DECISION (CC-0105): option (a) — keep operator + CR in "openstack". Reviewer: please verify.

issues_found.description: Negative-case test coverage for the dependsOn regression guard at tests/unit/deploy/helmrelease_namespaces_test.sh is thorough, but the suite as written does not assert that `deploy/flux-system/releases/keystone-operator.yaml` itself contains zero literal 'openstack' strings (analogous to the byte-level grep used for monitoring-system in Test 3). Adding such a guard would prevent a future edit from re-introducing `namespace: openstack` on a dependsOn entry that the typed yq query happens not to walk.
issues_found.fix: Add a belt-and-braces test that greps the rendered HelmRelease for any `namespace: openstack` reference and asserts a count of 0 (mirroring the monitoring-system guard in test_memcached_operator_dependsOn_monitoring).
issues_found.evidence: Lines 102-126 already implement this pattern for memcached-operator; the same pattern should apply to keystone-operator.yaml.

suggested_improvements: Run a final grep -rn 'keystone-operator.*-n openstack\|servicemonitor.*-n openstack\|helmrelease.*keystone-operator.*-n openstack' across hack/, tests/, docs/, and architecture/ to catch any remaining cross-reference audit misses. The four BLOCKING/MAJOR call sites above are exactly the kind the audit was designed to surface.

Consider adding a unit-level grep test (analogous to tests/unit/docs/namespace_consistency_test.sh but scoped to hack/ + tests/) that asserts no script or test still pins the keystone-operator HelmRelease/Deployment/Pod to the openstack Namespace. This would convert the current manual audit into an enforceable regression gate.
next_steps: 1) Fix hack/deploy-infra.sh:631,635 (-n openstack → -n keystone-system) and update tests/unit/hack/deploy_infra_prometheus_flag_test.sh:240 in the same commit.

2) Fix tests/e2e/keystone/prometheus-stack/chainsaw-test.yaml: namespace at line 298 (default → keystone-system), lines 307,311 (openstack → keystone-system), and rewrite the explanatory comment block at 287-297 to match the new ci-deploy-operator.sh contract.
4) Remove the stale DECISION comment at docs/reference/testing/chaos-e2e-tests.md:533.
6) Decide on `make kind-up` Makefile target vs revising REQ-017 (see TE1 finding).
7) Drop `Reviewer: please verify.` from tests/e2e/keystone/namespace-scoped-rbac/chainsaw-test.yaml:18 once the namespace-scoped-RBAC carve-out is confirmed.
8) After fixes, re-run the doc cross-reference audit (PR description Task 8.2) and append the additional fixed paths to the audit findings table.
